column4: "the cheapest call of duty game you can find"
column5: "Cheapest" is ambiguous without a defined data source, time context, or market scope — the AI cannot reliably calculate a real-time price and may hallucinate a value or cite stale pricing data as current fact.
column6: "Search [specified source, e.g., Steam, Amazon] for Call of Duty titles sorted by current price ascending, and return the lowest-priced result available today."

column5: Game titles have multiple official naming variants (e.g., "Call of Duty: Black Ops 4," "Black Ops IV," "COD BO4"; "Modern Warfare 2," "MW2," "Modern Warfare II," "Call of Duty: Modern Warfare 2 (2022)" vs. the original 2009 release) — a string-match exclusion on only the stated forms could silently fail to exclude all variants, including both the 2009 and 2022 versions of Modern Warfare 2.
column6: "Exclude any results matching the following titles or their known variants: 'Call of Duty: Black Ops 4' (also listed as Black Ops IV or BO4), 'Call of Duty: Modern Warfare 2' (both the 2009 and 2022 releases, also listed as MW2 or Modern Warfare II)."

column5: The instruction provides no defined data source or search scope, creating a logical gap — the AI has no authoritative, bounded dataset to query and may fabricate availability or pricing from training data rather than live market data.
column6: "Search the following sources for current pricing: [list specific platforms or APIs, e.g., Steam Store, CDKeys, Humble Bundle]. Return only results with a verifiable current price."

column4: "Show me the cheapest call of duty game you can find."
column5: The output format is entirely unspecified — the agent may return a paragraph of prose, a single title, a price list, or a recommendation with justification, with no way to determine which is correct behavior.
column6: "Return a single result in this format — Title: [full official title] | Platform: [platform name] | Price: [current price] | Source: [URL or store name]."

column5: This short prompt contains several compounding issues that could lead to silent failures or hallucinated data. The most critical risks are the lack of a bounded, real-time data source (meaning any price returned may be fabricated or outdated) and the underspecified exclusion list for game titles, which uses informal naming that could fail to match official or variant store listings — particularly dangerous given that "Modern Warfare 2" refers to two distinct games released 13 years apart. The absence of output format instructions and the vague "you can find" scope further increase the likelihood of inconsistent or unreliable agent behavior.
Show me the cheapest call of duty game you can find. I just don't want black ops 4 or modern warfare 2.

Search the Steam Store, Amazon, and CDKeys for Call of Duty titles currently available for purchase, sorted by price ascending. Return the single lowest-priced result available today.

Exclude any results matching the following titles or their known variants:
- "Call of Duty: Black Ops 4" (also listed as Black Ops IV or BO4)
- "Call of Duty: Modern Warfare 2" in any release — this includes both the original 2009 release and the 2022 release, and any listing using the names MW2, Modern Warfare II, or Modern Warfare 2 (2022)

Do not include any result whose price cannot be verified as a current, active listing on one of the specified platforms. Do not infer or estimate pricing from training data.

Return the result in exactly this format:
Title: [full official title] | Platform: [platform name] | Price: [current price in USD] | Source: [store name or URL]

column4: "currently available for purchase, sorted by price ascending"
column5: The agent cannot actually perform live web searches or access real-time store data unless given explicit tool access. "Currently available" implies real-time lookup, but this is not operationally defined — the agent has no verified mechanism to retrieve live pricing, and the instruction is written as if it can, creating a hallucination risk where the agent fabricates a plausible-sounding result.
column6: Add an explicit tool invocation step: "Use the [Search Tool / Browser Tool] to query Steam Store, Amazon, and CDKeys in real time. If any store cannot be queried, exclude it and note this in your response."

column4: "Call of Duty: Modern Warfare 2" in any release — this includes both the original 2009 release and the 2022 release, and any listing using the names MW2, Modern Warfare II, or Modern Warfare 2 (2022)"
column5: The exclusion list relies on string matching across highly inconsistent real-world listing titles. Store listings may use stylizations like "Call of Duty®: Modern Warfare® 2" (with registered trademark symbols), "CoD MW2", "COD: MW II", or locale-specific punctuation and diacritics, none of which are covered by the listed variants and could silently pass the filter.
column6: "Exclude any listing where the title contains any of these substrings (case-insensitive, ignoring symbols and punctuation): 'modern warfare 2', 'modern warfare ii', 'mw2', 'mwii'. Apply fuzzy matching across trademark symbols, punctuation, and spacing variations."

column4: "Call of Duty: Black Ops 4" (also listed as Black Ops IV or BO4)"
column5: Listings may also appear as "COD: BO4", "Black Ops 4 (2018)", "Call of Duty®: Black Ops 4", or with regional subtitle differences, none of which are explicitly covered. A listing with a registered trademark symbol or year suffix could silently bypass the exclusion.
column6: "Exclude any listing where the title contains 'black ops 4', 'black ops iv', or 'bo4' (case-insensitive, ignoring punctuation and trademark symbols)."

column4: "Return the single lowest-priced result available today."
column5: The exclusion logic and the selection logic are written in separate blocks with no explicit bridge. After applying exclusions, the agent may re-sort or re-evaluate across all three platforms without a clear tie-breaking or deduplication rule — e.g., if the same title appears on two platforms at the same price, the agent has no instruction on which source to prefer or whether to return both.
column6: "After applying all exclusions, return the single remaining result with the lowest price. If two results share the same lowest price, prefer the result from the first listed platform (Steam > Amazon > CDKeys) and note that a tie exists."

column4: "Return the result in exactly this format: Title: [full official title] | Platform: [platform name] | Price: [current price in USD] | Source: [store name or URL]"
column5: The format template uses bracket placeholders like "[full official title]" which an LLM may interpret as literal output tokens to preserve or may inconsistently omit brackets vs. fill them. There is also no instruction about what to output if no valid result is found after exclusions, leaving the format undefined for a null result.
column6: Provide a filled example: "Format your answer exactly as shown: Title: Call of Duty: Vanguard | Platform: PC | Price: $4.99 | Source: CDKeys.com — Do not include brackets. If no valid result is found, output: NO VALID RESULT FOUND and explain why."

column4: "Do not infer or estimate pricing from training data."
column5: This instruction correctly guards against hallucination but creates a logical paradox: the prompt does not explicitly grant the agent any tool access or retrieval mechanism, meaning the only available data source is training data. If the agent has no real-time tool, following this instruction correctly would mean it cannot return any result — but the prompt simultaneously demands one, setting up a silent failure where the agent either ignores the constraint or fabricates data anyway.
column6: Add a fallback instruction: "If real-time store access is unavailable, respond with: 'Unable to retrieve live pricing. Please run this query using a browser-enabled agent or provide current store data.' Do not return any price values derived from training data."

column5: This prompt has a fundamental operational paradox at its core: it demands real-time pricing data from live storefronts while simultaneously prohibiting inference from training data, yet never grants or confirms access to any retrieval tool. This creates a high-severity hallucination trap that undermines the entire prompt's purpose. Compounding this, the exclusion logic is vulnerable to silent literal matching failures due to inconsistent real-world listing formats (trademark symbols, alternate abbreviations, year suffixes) that are not covered by the listed variants. The formatting template is ambiguous for edge cases like ties or null results, and the separation of exclusion and selection logic introduces a scope leakage risk in how the final result is chosen. Together, these issues make it likely that an agent executing this prompt would either silently return an excluded title, fabricate plausible-sounding pricing, or produce malformed output with no indication that anything went wrong.
